facts.text: Comic books combine sequential art and text to tell stories through panels, captions, and dialogue balloons.
Action Comics #1, published in 1938, introduced Superman and is often considered the beginning of the superhero boom.
Detective Comics #27 from 1939 introduced Batman, helping cement DC's early dominance in superhero comics.
The late 1930s and 1940s are known as the Golden Age of comic books.
The Silver Age of comics is often dated from the mid-1950s through the early 1970s and saw major revivals of superheroes.
Marvel's rise in the 1960s came from co-created characters like Spider-Man, the Fantastic Four, the X-Men, Hulk, and the Avengers.
Stan Lee, Jack Kirby, Steve Ditko, Will Eisner, and many other creators shaped the language of modern comic storytelling.
The Comics Code Authority was formed in 1954 and heavily restricted what mainstream American comics could depict for decades.
Graphic novel is a publishing format rather than a genre — it can include memoir, horror, fantasy, journalism, or superhero stories.
Will Eisner's A Contract with God helped popularize the term 'graphic novel' in the late 1970s.
Maus by Art Spiegelman became the first graphic novel to win a Pulitzer Prize.
Watchmen and The Dark Knight Returns helped push superhero comics toward darker, more complex storytelling in the 1980s.
Comic shops often sell monthly 'floppy' issues, collected trade paperbacks, hardcovers, and omnibus editions.
A key issue is a comic valued especially highly because it contains a major first appearance, death, origin, or landmark event.
Collectors often preserve comic books in bags and boards to protect them from light, moisture, and wear.
Third-party grading companies encapsulate high-value comics in plastic slabs with condition scores.
Amazing Fantasy #15, the first appearance of Spider-Man, is one of the most famous and valuable comic books ever printed.
Comic crossovers became a major sales driver, bringing different heroes and teams together in the same storyline.
Crisis on Infinite Earths, Secret Wars, and Infinity Gauntlet are among the most influential event comics of the modern era.
Manga is the Japanese comic tradition and has become one of the biggest forces in global comics publishing.
Tintin, Asterix, and other Franco-Belgian comics helped make Europe another major center of comic-book culture.
San Diego Comic-Con began as a comic-book convention and grew into one of the world's biggest pop-culture events.
Independent publishers like Image, Dark Horse, Boom!, and IDW expanded the American comics landscape beyond Marvel and DC.
Image Comics was founded in 1992 by superstar artists who wanted creators to own their work.
The Walking Dead, Invincible, Saga, Hellboy, and Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles all helped prove comics can thrive beyond classic superhero formulas.
Comic book adaptations now dominate much of the box office, but many influential comics remain known mainly in print.
Many artists still draw or ink pages by hand, while others work entirely digitally using tablets and illustration software.
Lettering, coloring, penciling, inking, and editing are all distinct crafts that shape how a comic reads on the page.
A comic's cover can become as iconic as the story itself, especially for first appearances and major event issues.
Comic books have influenced film, TV, games, toys, fashion, and fandom culture for generations.
Newspaper comic strips helped lay the groundwork for modern comic books long before superheroes dominated the medium.
Funnies on Parade from 1933 is often cited as one of the earliest modern American comic books.
EC Comics became legendary for horror and science-fiction titles like Tales from the Crypt and Weird Science.
Public concern over violent and horror comics in the 1950s led to U.S. Senate hearings and the creation of the Comics Code Authority.
The underground comix movement of the 1960s and 1970s rejected censorship and embraced more personal, political, and adult storytelling.
The direct market system let specialty comic shops order nonreturnable comics, changing how publishers sold books starting in the 1970s.
Free Comic Book Day is held on the first Saturday in May and brings new readers into shops with special giveaway issues.
The Eisner Awards are often called the comic industry's equivalent of the Oscars.
The Harvey Awards are named after Mad co-creator Harvey Kurtzman and celebrate excellence in comics and graphic storytelling.
Webcomics opened the door for creators to publish directly online without needing a traditional print publisher.
Korean comics are often called manhwa, and many are now published in vertical-scroll digital formats known as webtoons.
Manga volumes in Japan are commonly collected in book form as tankobon.
Franco-Belgian comics like Tintin and Asterix are often published in large, beautifully designed album editions.
Jack Kirby co-created or helped shape a huge portion of the Marvel Universe, earning him the nickname 'The King.'
Steve Ditko co-created Spider-Man and Doctor Strange, two of Marvel's most enduring characters.
The Sandman by Neil Gaiman helped show that comics could be literary, philosophical, and deeply mythic without fitting neatly into superhero formulas.
Bone by Jeff Smith became one of the most acclaimed independent fantasy comics ever published.
Saga by Brian K. Vaughan and Fiona Staples became a major creator-owned hit by blending space opera, romance, and family drama.
Invincible began as a comic book before becoming one of the most popular adult animated superhero series on streaming.
Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles started as a black-and-white independent comic before growing into a giant multimedia franchise.
Variant covers are alternate front covers for the same issue and can become major collector items.
Comic book letterers do far more than place words on a page — they shape pacing, tone, readability, and sound effects.
Digital coloring transformed the look of mainstream comics in the 1990s and 2000s, allowing for more complex lighting and texture.
Trade paperbacks helped comics reach bookstores and libraries, bringing many readers into the medium through collected editions.
Libraries now play a major role in comics culture by stocking graphic novels for kids, teens, and adults.
Many comic readers discover major stories through collected runs and omnibuses rather than monthly single issues.
The Yellow Kid is often cited as one of the earliest newspaper comic-strip phenomena in American pop culture.
Will Eisner's The Spirit helped show how inventive page layouts and cinematic storytelling could transform comics.
Peanuts, Calvin and Hobbes, and other newspaper strips influenced generations of cartoonists even outside comic-book publishing.
Alan Moore's work on Watchmen, Swamp Thing, and V for Vendetta changed how many readers thought about comics as a literary medium.
Art Spiegelman's Maus used anthropomorphic animals to tell a deeply personal Holocaust story and changed the reputation of graphic novels.
Neil Gaiman's The Sandman blended mythology, horror, fantasy, and literature in a way that expanded the audience for comics.
Brian Bolland's cover for Batman: The Killing Joke became one of the most famous comic-book images ever published.
The long white storage boxes seen in comic shops are a familiar sight for collectors organizing back issues.
A comic 'gutter' is the space between panels, and readers mentally fill in action there — one of the medium's most unique tricks.
Scott McCloud's Understanding Comics became one of the most influential books ever written about how comics actually work.
The term 'sequential art' is often associated with Will Eisner's efforts to describe comics as a legitimate artistic form.
Shonen manga is generally aimed at younger male readers, while shojo manga traditionally targets younger female readers.
Webtoon-style vertical scrolling changed how comics are read on phones by making the page less important than the screen flow.
Independent minicomic creators often publish small handmade or self-printed comics before ever working with a larger publisher.
Comics criticism, scholarship, and archiving have grown so much that universities now regularly teach graphic-novel and comics studies courses.

It remains one of the most famous and valuable comic books ever printed.
